summary: No material scope-bypass, CSRF-state bypass, or token-in-URL leak was found, but the OAuth callback has a material correctness flaw: it reads client-id/client-secret positionally from a helper that sorts by key name, so a new connector can silently swap those credentials and fail token exchange.

findings.title: OAuth callback assumes scoped secret order, so client_id and client_secret can be swapped for connectors whose secret names sort differently
findings.recommendation: Do not destructure `scopedSecrets(...)` by array position. Resolve the returned secrets by `key` (or change `scopedSecrets` to preserve requested order) before building the token-exchange request, otherwise new OAuth connectors with differently named secrets will fail with `client_credentials_missing`, `token_exchange_*`, or persist the wrong client metadata.
